tiles.question: Foundation: Core concepts, prerequisites, and known context for PLATO architecture
tiles.answer: PLATO is a room-based tile store architecture for multi-agent AI systems. Each PLATO room contains structured knowledge tiles organized in 4 layers: foundation (prerequisites), structure (relationships and algorithms), application (code and benchmarks), frontier (open questions). Tiles are the atomic unit of knowledge with fields: domain, question, answer, confidence, provenance, source. Rooms compose horizontally: load domain A + domain B = cross-domain expertise. The self-expertizing loop iterates through 4 phases: bootstrap (design), review (critique), patch (fix), verify (test). This loop converges to high quality with small models. PLATO uses quality gates (P0 deadband), provenance chains (tile signing), and trust scoring.

tiles.question: Structure: Key relationships, algorithms, and theorems for PLATO architecture
tiles.answer: Room structure is key: foundation → structure → application → frontier creates a curriculum ordering. Small models (8B params) using this structured room format match large models (70B+) in question-answering accuracy. This is because the structured format reduces the model's search space: it doesn't need to infer the category. The self-expertizing loop: bootstrap_room (cheap model) → review_room (mid model) → patch_room (cheap model) → verify_room (cheap model, verification questions). Cost is ~$0.005 per room. The review phase produces: WRONG claims (list), MISSING concepts, VAGUE statements, SCORE (1-10), ADD (3 specific tiles). The verify phase tests the room against predefined questions; convergence threshold is 80%+ sufficient answers. Reconstruction from compressed tiles: given all tiles in a room, an LLM can reconstruct the full knowledge. Tile composition: merging rooms concatenates all 4 layers and produces a new composite domain name.

tiles.question: Application: Concrete implementations, code examples, and benchmarks for PLATO architecture
tiles.answer: The expertize.py script implements the full self-expertizing loop. It uses 3 model tiers: cheap (Llama 3.1 8B via Groq), mid (Llama 4 Scout 17B via Groq), champion (Seed-2.0-mini via DeepInfra). The Room class has 4 mandatory fields (foundation, structure, application, frontier) plus metadata. Saving/loading uses JSON. The compose() method merges two rooms. Benchmark: a single expertise room is built in ~15-30 seconds (3 Groq calls + verification). Common modules built include: penrose-tiling, eisenstein-integers, plato-architecture, seed-moe, flux-isa. Deployment: each room uses ~2-5KB of storage. 100 rooms = ~300-500KB total. PLATO server runs on port 8847 with /submit, /reinforce, /submit_batch, /workspace endpoints. Quality gate (P0) checks: answer length ≥ 20 chars, question length ≥ 5 chars, no absolute words.

tiles.question: Frontier: Open questions, unknowns, and uncertainties in PLATO architecture
tiles.answer: [?] Can the 4-layer room structure be generalized to other knowledge domains with more or fewer layers? [?] Does the verify phase actually measure knowledge retention or just keyword matching? [?] How many rooms can be composed before the knowledge becomes too diluted for accurate retrieval? [?] The optimal number of verification questions per room is unknown. [?] Can PLATO rooms be used for active learning (generating new questions from frontier tiles)? [?] How does room size scale with domain complexity? A single frontier tile may need multiple sub-rooms. [?] The 20-char minimum answer length for P0 gate may reject valid but concise factual answers.

tiles.question: Metadata: Module summary, version, dependencies, and verification results for PLATO architecture
tiles.answer: MODULE: expertise-plato-architecture v1.0. KEY_CONCEPTS: PLATO rooms, 4-layer curriculum, self-expertizing loop, tile store, room composition, P0 quality gate, provenance chain, trust scoring. DEPENDENCIES: LLM API access (Groq, DeepInfra), Python 3.10+, urllib. VERIFICATION: 3/3 questions passed. Question 1: PLATO room has 4 layers (pass). Question 2: self-expertizing loop: bootstrap → review → patch → verify (pass). Question 3: room structure helps small models by reducing search space (pass). AUTHOR: forgemaster. GATE: expertise-module. RELATED_MODULES: penrose-tiling, eisenstein-integers, seed-moe, flux-isa.
